prompt:
Develop a CivicOps Operations & Services Management system for a local organization that runs a physical lost-and-found desk, manages equipment assets, coordinates volunteers, and sells in-person photography packages, all within an offline facility network. The backend, built in Rust using Actix-web for API delivery and Diesel with PostgreSQL for persistence, shall expose resource-based interface groups for Authentication and Session APIs, Lost-and-Found APIs, Asset Lifecycle APIs, Volunteer Qualification APIs, Photography Package APIs, Notification Center APIs, and Administration/RBAC APIs. Lost-and-found capabilities must support create/update/unpublish/delete, draft→in_review→published workflows, and standardized occurrence fields including event_date (MM/DD/YYYY), event_time (12-hour with AM/PM), and location_text (max 200 chars; no map lookups). Each record must allow categories (single enum) and tags (0–10, each 2–24 chars), and attachments (images/PDF) up to 10 files totaling 25 MB; images must be compressed locally to a maximum 1920 px on the long edge and stored with a SHA-256 fingerprint to prevent duplicates. Asset lifecycle capabilities must enforce a state machine covering intake, assignment, loan, transfer, maintenance, repair, inventory_count, and disposal, with bulk actions limited to 500 assets per request and requiring a single target state; barcode/QR label values must be unique per facility. Photography package capabilities must support CRUD, publish/unpublish, pricing in USD with two decimals, included items, optional variants (up to 20 combinations per package), and optional linkage to internal inventory items or time slots without external calendars. The Notification & Messaging Center must provide in-app messages plus offline “outbox” records for email/SMS/webhook that can be exported for later sending; templating must support variable rendering from approved fields and trigger at submission/supplement/review/change events, with send logs, retry up to 3 times, and per-user subscription preferences.

Data design shall include normalized tables for users, roles, permissions, stores/facilities, audit_logs, lost_found_items, attachments, assets, asset_events, maintenance_records, volunteers, qualifications, packages, package_variants, notifications, and outbox_deliveries; primary keys use UUID, timestamps use local time with stored offset, and high-selectivity indexes must exist on facility_id + status, asset_label, and created_at. RBAC must support fine-grained permission-to-resource mapping including data scope (single facility vs all facilities) and field-level access control; sensitive fields (government ID numbers, certificates, private volunteer notes) must be encrypted at rest and masked by default (e.g., show last 4 characters). Authentication is local username + password only, with password minimum 12 characters, salted hashing, lockout after 5 failed attempts in 15 minutes, and session expiration at 8 hours idle. All write operations must be idempotent via a client-provided request_id (UUID) retained for 24 hours per user. The system must run as a single Docker-deployed service on one machine with no third-party network dependencies, support at least 50 concurrent users and 200 requests/second on commodity hardware, record immutable audit trails for admin actions, and provide structured logs and health diagnostics suitable for offline incident review.
